coredata.dc:title: Metal-organic framework-based ratiometric point-of-care testing for quantitative visual detection of nitrite
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Maosheng
Gao, Yifan
Sun, Xiaoling
Kong, Rong-Mei
Xia, Lian
Qu, Fengli
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite (NO2 −) is categorized as a carcinogenic substance and is subjected to severe limitations in water and food. To safeguard the public’s health, developing fast and convenient methods for determination of NO2 − is of significance. Point-of-care testing (POCT) affords demotic measurement of NO2 − and shows huge potential in future technology beyond those possible with traditional methods. Here, a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (Ru@MOF-NH2) is developed by integrating UiO-66-NH2 with tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) ([Ru(bpy)3]2+) through a one-pot approach. The special diazo-reaction between the amino group of UiO-66-NH2 and NO2 − is responsible for the report signal (blue emission) with high selectivity and the red emission from [Ru(bpy)3]2+ offers the reference signal. The proposed probe shows obviously distinguishable color change from blue to red towards NO2 − via naked-eye. Moreover, using a smartphone as the detection device to read color hue, ultra-sensitive quantitative detection of NO2 − is achieved with a low limit of detection at 0.6 μΜ. The accuracy and repeatability determined in spiked samples through quantitative visualization is in the range of 105 to 117% with a coefficient of variation below 4.3%. This POCT sensing platform presents a promising strategy for detecting NO2 − and expands the potential applications for on-site monitoring in food and environment safety assessment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Point-of-care testing
Ratiometric fluorescence
Metal-organic framework
Nitrite
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of Ru@MOF-NH2 for detecting NO2 −
Detection of nitrite in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by grants awarded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174086, 22074080), Outstanding Taishan Scholar of Shandong Province (tsqn202211131), Natural Science Foundation Projects of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB056, ZR2020KB020), Outstanding Youth Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017JL010) and Changjiang Scholar Program of the Ministry of Education of China (Q2019258), Open Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (2021014). All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

This work is supported by grants awarded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174086, 22074080), Outstanding Taishan Scholar of Shandong Province (tsqn202211131), Natural Science Foundation Projects of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB056, ZR2020KB020), Youth Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017JL010) and Changjiang Scholar Program of the Ministry of Education of China (Q2019258), Open foundation of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (2021014).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the (A) MOF-NH2 and (B) Ru@MOF-NH2. (C) XRD patterns comparison of MOF-NH2, Ru@MOF-NH2 and the simulated form. High resolution XPS: (D) C 1 s, (E) Zr 3d, (F) N 1 s of MOF-NH2 (top) and Ru@MOF-NH2 (below). (G) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MOF-NH2 (top) and Ru@MOF-NH2. (H) UV–vis spectra, and (I) fluorescence spectra of MOF-NH2, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and Ru@MOF-NH2.

(A) Emission (Em) spectrum with λEx = 360 nm (black) and excitation (Ex) spectrum with λEm = 474 nm (red) of Ru@MOF-NH2 (0.2 mg mL−1) with presence of sulfuric acid (0.06 M). (B) The contrast fluorescence spectra of Ru@MOF-NH2 with or without NO2 − (10 μM) under excitation of 360 nm. Inset photographs: before (left) and after (right) adding NO2 − ions under ultraviolet. (C) Griess test results: the UV–vis response of Ru@MOF-NH2 (550 nm) to various of NO2 − with adding N-(1-naphthalene) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (10 mg mL−1). Inset: photographs under natural light. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of Ru@MOF-NH2 with and without reacting with NO2 −.

(A) The fluorescence of Ru@MOF-NH2 (0.2 mg mL−1) with response to various concentrations of NO2 −. (B) The fluorescent images of Ru@MOF-NH2 after reaction with different amount of NO2 − under UV radiation of 365 nm. (C) The fitting curve of the ratio values of F474/F608 versus the NO2 − concentration. (D) NO2 − triggered CIE chromaticity coordinates of Ru@MOF-NH2 with irradiation of 365 nm.

(A) Relationship of the colorimetric intensity (R/B) value versus NO2 − concentration. (B) Accuracy and repeatability of visual detection. (C) The sensing performance comparison of the proposed method with of other different methods for visualization and quantification. (cyan, orange, and green dots: lab-instrument-based methods, blue circles: other POCT methods, purple circle: only visualization method, red star: this work).

Selectivity of the sensing platform against other interferences. Concentrations of NO2 − and other interferences were 100 μM and 1.0 mM, respectively (n = 3). (A) Fluorescent ratio values of F474/F608 toward NO2 − and interferences; (B) R/B values toward NO2 − and interferences (C) The photographs of the sensing system towards NO2 − and interferences under 365 nm UV lamp.
Schematic diagrams of the synthesis of Ru@MOF-NH2 and smartphone readout for point-of-care testing of NO2 −.
The performance of the developed sensing platform for detection of NO2 − in lake water and ham sausage (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Metal-organic framework-based ratiometric point-of-care testing for quantitative visual detection of nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering College, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering College, Qufu Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering College, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is categorized as a carcinogenic substance and is subjected to severe limitations in water and food. To safeguard the public’s health, developing fast and convenient methods for determination of NO2 − is of significance. Point-of-care testing (POCT) affords demotic measurement of NO2 − and shows huge potential in future technology beyond those possible with traditional methods. Here, a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (Ru@MOF-NH2) is developed by integrating UiO-66-NH2 with tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) ([Ru(bpy)3]2+) through a one-pot approach. The special diazo-reaction between the amino group of UiO-66-NH2 and NO2 − is responsible for the report signal (blue emission) with high selectivity and the red emission from [Ru(bpy)3]2+ offers the reference signal. The proposed probe shows obviously distinguishable color change from blue to red towards NO2 − via naked-eye. Moreover, using a smartphone as the detection device to read color hue, ultra-sensitive quantitative detection of NO2 − is achieved with a low limit of detection at 0.6 μΜ. The accuracy and repeatability determined in spiked samples through quantitative visualization is in the range of 105 to 117% with a coefficient of variation below 4.3%. This POCT sensing platform presents a promising strategy for detecting NO2 − and expands the potential applications for on-site monitoring in food and environment safety assessment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is widely used in many fields, including as an antiseptic and flavoring agent in food processing industry, as a nitrogen-based fertilizer in agriculture and as a raw material in industry [1–3]. But excessive NO2 − in waters and foods is extremely harmful to public health, which easily leads to methemoglobinemia, accidental intoxications or cause intestinal cancer or gastric carcinoma [4,5]. It is important to mention that NO2 – has been categorized as a carcinogenic substance by the World Health Organization (WHO) and is subjected to limitations imposed by global regulatory bodies. Different countries in the world have set different limitation standards for NO2 – in drinking water. The revised Chinese national standard stipulates that the maximum content of nitrite in drinking water is 0.1 mg/L (NO2 –). The Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and most countries have a nitrite limit of 3.0 mg/L (NO2 -) for drinking water and packaged drinking water. Specially, the European Union has set stricter limitations for nitrite in drinking water and packaged drinking water of 0.50 mg/L (NO2 –). Owing to the widespread application and the high toxicity of NO2 −, developing point-of-care testing (POCT) methods for convenient detection of NO2 − with straightforward signal readout is quite necessary for food safety and public health.

However, at present, NO2 − detection mainly depends on traditional methods including chromatography [6,7], electrochemistry [8,9], UV spectrophotometry [10,11], and spectrofluorimetry methods [12,13]. Each kind of methods have their own advantages and disadvantages. For example, chromatography methods offer high sensitivity but require sophisticated instruments, professional operators and complex derivatization process; electrochemistry methods have satisfactory sensitivity but most of them can hardly applied for real sample analysis because they are always susceptible to interferences from complex samples; the most widely used UV spectrophotometry methods for nitrite analysis is the Griess Assay, however, this kind of methods are time-consuming and has low sensitivity due to interference by other ions, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, S2− and Cl− [14]. In comparison, spectrofluorimetry methods are by far the most potential methods for the determination of NO2 − due to their simple operation and stable readout-signal. However, spectrofluorimetry methods with single-signal are easily influenced by the external factors and result in false results [15,16]. In comparison, ratiometric technique offers a reference signal to obviously reduce interference and enhance accuracy via self-calibration [17–19]. In addition, ratiometric tests usually produce obviously distinguishable color change and thus easily perform visual detection and color recognition [20–23]. Unfortunately, at present, most spectrofluorimetry methods for NO2 − determination are based on some organic fluorescent molecules, which not only needed complex synthesis procedures but also tedious purification process. Most importantly, it is more difficult to realize ratiometric detection using a single fluorescent molecule. Therefore, developing nano-probes with ratiometric fluorescence to determinate NO2 − shows more attractions to the researchers. Although, several ratiometric fluorescent sensors have been constructed for detection of NO2 − in food [24–28]. Most of them were using carbon dots as the probes, which showed similar color hue between the two fluorescent signals and therefore were difficultly distinguished via naked-eye. It is necessary to develop ratiometric nano-probes for NO2 − determination with obvious color change and large Stocks shift. Thanks to the Luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs), which show intrinsic optical physical property and porosity for guest-accommodating ability, are promising for fluorescent sensor applications [29–31]. Therefore, accommodating reference-signal probe by active-groups-functionalized LMOFs to construct a ratiometric probe to monitor NO2 − is most possible.

Herein, a promising ratiometric fluorescent sensor for NO2 − detection was developed by embedding [Ru(bpy)3]2+ into an amino-functionalized LMOF (MOF-NH2) through a facile one-pot synthesis approach ( Scheme 1). The resulted sensor denotes as Ru@MOF-NH2 in the following. The MOF-NH2 (UiO-66-NH2), a amino group functionalized MOF with characteristic of high chemical stability, shows blue fluorescence emission at 474 nm, acting as the response probe. [Ru(bpy)3]2+, a new luminescent reagent, emits stable red fluorescence at 608 nm, providing reference signal. The amino group of MOF-NH2 can react with NO2 − under strong acidic condition with high specificity and sensitivity based on the diazo reaction, producing diazonium salts and hence quenching the blue fluorescence emission. With adding NO2 − into the sensing system (acid aqueous solution of the Ru@MOF-NH2), the blue fluorescence emission from MOF-NH2 decreases gradually with the increase of NO2 − concentration, whereas the red fluorescent emission from [Ru(bpy)3]2+ remains constant, making for distinguishable color changes from blue to purple and finally to red. Using a smartphone-based analyzer, ultrasensitive quantitative visual analysis of NO2
− is achieved by reading R/B values.

Ru@MOF-NH2 was prepared through a facile one-pot synthesis approach [32]. Briefly, zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 0.08 g), BDC-NH2 (0.062 g) and Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6 H2O (0.015 g) were successively dissolved in DMF (30 mL). After 5 min of ultrasonication to be homogeneous, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, then maintained at 120 ℃ for 24 h in an oven. After the reaction accomplishing, the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature, then orange powder was obtained through centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min). The resulted product was first washed with DMF for three times to remove the redundant reactants, then with EtOH for another three time to get rid of DMF. Finally, the product was dried at 60 ℃ overnight in vacuo to get Ru@MOF-NH2. When not in use, the Ru@MOF-NH2 was stored in a desiccator.
The preparation procedure of MOF-NH2 was similar to that of Ru@MOF-NH2, just lack of the Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6 H2O.

A suspension of Ru@MOF-NH2 nanoparticles was prepared by dispersing 0.02 g Ru@MOF-NH2 powder into 100 mL ultrapure water with ultrasonication for 20 min. Then the NO2 − determination using Ru@MOF-NH2 as the sensing probe was performed. Briefly, deionized water (700 μL), sulfuric acid solution (0.06 M, 100 μL), Ru@MOF-NH2 solution (0.2 mg mL−1, 100 μL) and different concentrations of NO2 − solution (100 μL) were added orderly into a test tube and allowed for reacting 30 min. The resulting solution was moved into a quartz cell and conducted fluorescence emission spectra measurement under the excitation of 360 nm.

In order to guarantee the consistency of the light source for taking photos to minimize the interferences, the UV light was set in a dark box with a fixed angle and position. Under the UV light irradiation, the images of the sensing solutions were taken with a smartphone camera, and then the images were analyzed via the Swatches App (many other Apps, such as ColorPicker can be used) of smartphone to obtain R/B values (shown in Fig. S1). With the establishment of the standard curve of the R/B value versus NO2 − concentration, the content of NO2 − in the sample can be obtained according to the standard linear equation based on the R/B value reading on the smartphone App.

The selectivity study was performed according to the following processes. Several interference substances such as NaCl, KBr, KI, MgSO4, NaCO3, Pb(NO3)2, CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2, MnCl2, FeCl3, CuSO4, AlCl3, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, glucose, ascorbic acid, phenylalanine, cysteine, L-histidine, glycine, tyrosine, arginine, and glutamate were individually prepared as solutions of 10 mM. Then using Ru@MOF-NH2 (0.2 mg mL−1) as the sensing probe was performed. To put it simply, deionized water (700 μL), sulfuric acid solution (100 μL), Ru@MOF-NH2 solution (100 μL) and different interference substances solution (100 μL) were added orderly into a test tube and allowed for reacting 30 min, then performed fluorescence and color hue test. In addition, the control group was set with the mixture of 100 μL Ru@MOF-NH2 solution, 100 μL sulfuric acid solution NO2 − solution (1 mM) and 700 μL water.
All samples with or without spiking NO2 − standard were analyzed with the same procedure of the Sections 2.2 and 2.3 after sample preparation.

The practicality of Ru@MOF-NH2 as the NO2 − sensing probe was evaluated by analyzing lake water and ham sausage. Lake water was collected from Qufu Normal University Lake and ham sausage was purchased from the local market.

The ham sausage sample was cut into small pieces, 0.5 g of the sample was added to 10 mL of CH3OH, and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 40 min. After being centrifuged for 15 min (12,000 rpm), the supernatant was collected. This procedure was repeated thrice for all samples. The combined supernatant was dried with nitrogen and re-dissolved into 3 mL of water to wait for quantitative analysis. The lake water samples were prepared by centrifugation and filtered with 0.45 µm membranes to remove impurities and waited for quantitative analysis.
The recoveries were determined by spiking the samples with four different concentrations of the standard solutions (3, 5,10,15 μM).
All samples with or without spiking NO2 − standard were analyzed with the same procedure of the Sections 2.2 and 2.3 after sample preparation.

Ru@MOF-NH2 was synthesized through a facile one-pot approach with a slight modification for the synthesis of MOF-NH2. Fig. 1A and B show the TEM images of MOF-NH2 and Ru@MOF-NH2, respectively. From Fig. 1A and B, we can see that the MOF-NH2 formed an octahedral structure with an average size of 80 nm, whereas the Ru@MOF-NH2 showed a similar structure with the pristine MOF-NH2. The PXRD patterns of MOF-NH2 with and without embedding [Ru(bpy)3]2+ were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 1C. According to Fig. 1C, the PXRD patterns of MOF-NH2 were consistent with that of the simulated one, suggesting the successful synthesis of MOF-NH2. Most importantly, the high consistency of diffraction peaks between Ru@MOF-NH2 and MOF-NH2 revealed that the introduction of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ caused no crystal structure change on MOF-NH2. The representative peaks of the MOF-NH2 at 7.4° and 8.5°, corresponding to the (111) and (200) crystal planes, were both observed in MOF-NH2 and Ru@MOF-NH2, further indicating the crystal structure was not damaged during the fabrication. The composition of Ru@MOF-NH2 was tested by EDS and XPS. EDS spectrum (Fig. S2) confirmed the existence of Ru in the Ru@MOF-NH2. As for the XPS data, the band energy of C 1 s overlapped with that of Ru 3d (Fig. S3) [33]. Therefore, in C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ru@MOF-NH2, apart from three characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV (C-C), 285.1 eV (C-O) and 288.6 eV (O-CO), a new peak at 284.2 eV attributed to Ru 3d3/2 was also observed (Fig. 1D), indicating that the presentation of Ru didn’t influence the coordination environment of the MOF-NH2. Zeta potential measurements showed that MOF-NH2 and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ had opposite zeta potentials of 48.00 mV and − 8.06 mV in aqueous solution. After adulterating [Ru(bpy)3]2+, the resultant Ru@MOF-NH2 showed an eclectic zeta potential of 33.00 mV (Fig. S4). The porosity of MOF-NH2 and Ru@MOF-NH2 were evaluated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm. As shown in Fig. 1G, after loading with [Ru(bpy)3]2+, the resultant Ru@MOF-NH2 showed obvious decrease in N2 adsorption volume. In addition, BET surface areas of MOF-NH2 and Ru@MOF-NH2 were determined to be 769 m2 g−1 and 42 m2 g−1, respectively. These results suggested that [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was embedded not only on the surface but also in the pores of MOF-NH2.

Optical properties of Ru@MOF-NH2 were evaluated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1H, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ showed two obvious absorption bands at 260 nm and 450 nm (blue curve), whereas the MOF-NH2 showed adsorption at 360 nm. After their combination, the resulted composite of Ru@MOF-NH2 only obviously showed the two characteristic absorption peaks of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 260 nm and 450 nm (red curve). The reason was that the adsorption of MOF-NH2 was inconspicuous comparing with the ultra-strong absorption of [Ru(bpy)3]2+. Fluorescence of [Ru(bpy)3]2+, MOF-NH2 and Ru@MOF-NH2 were evaluated respectively with the excitation at 360 nm. As shown in Fig. 1I, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ showed its characteristic fluorescence-emission at 608 nm and the MOF-NH2 showed fluorescence-emission at 474 nm. As expectation, Ru@MOF-NH2 exhibited a dual-emitting at 474 and 608 nm simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of Ru@MOF-NH2 for detecting NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the excellent fluorescence and the amino group function of the Ru@MOF-NH2, as well as the principle that aromatic amine can be diazotized by acidified NO2 − to produce diazonium salt, we were inspired to investigate the feasibility of sensing NO2 − using Ru@MOF-NH2 as the fluorescent probe under the acid condition. The excitation and emission wavelength of Ru@MOF-NH2 were firstly measured, and the results were shown in Fig. 2A. With the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, Ru@MOF-NH2 emitted a dual-emission at 474 and 608 nm. With adding 10 μM NO2 − into the system, the amino group of MOF-NH2 reacted with NO2 − to produce diazonium salts with high specificity and sensitivity based on the diazo reaction, therefore the fluorescent emission of Ru@MOF-NH2 at 474 nm (from MOF-NH2) was quenched as high as 50%, whereas that at 608 (from Ru) remained constant (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, obvious color variation from blue to purple was simultaneously observed with naked-eye under UV light at 365 nm (inset paragraph of Fig. 2B). Griess test was carried out to confirm the diazo reaction taking place. As shown in Fig. 2C, the Ru@MOF-NH2 couldn’t cause color change without adding NO2 −, whereas with adding NO2 − into the sensing system (Ru@MOF-NH2) in the presence of the Griess reagent of N-(1-naphthalene) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride, obvious purple color was observed. Moreover, with increasing NO2 − concentration, the color changed from slight purple to dark purple and the UV–vis absorption intensity at 550 nm enhanced gradually. This indicated that the diazo-reaction occurred between the Ru@MOF-NH2 and NO2 −. The detailed process of the reaction is shown in Fig. S5. Fig. 2D exhibited the comparison of fluorescence decay curves with and without adding NO2 −. According to the results of Fig. 2D, after reaction with NO2 −, the fluorescence lifetime of the probe shifted from 12.46 to 6.44 ns, suggesting the dynamic quenching mechanism and the occurrence of diazo-reaction. XRD patterns of Ru@MOF-NH2 before and after reacting with NO2 − (Fig. S6) demonstrated that the MOF kept its crystalline structure integrity after the sensing performance, suggesting the structural stability of the probe and the fluorescence quenching was irrelevant to the collapse of framework. The day-to-day fluorescence stability of Ru@MOF-NH2 was also investigated (Fig. S10), the results showed that the probe exhibited highly day-to-day fluorescence stability in 7 days under the sensing condition. These results indicates that the probe of Ru@MOF-NH2 is stable enough during the sensing process.

To test the fluorescence titration of NO2 − using Ru@MOF-NH2 as the sensing probe, sensing conditions were optimized (Fig. S7). Under the optimized conditions, the Ru@MOF-NH2 was incubated with different concentrations of NO2 − (0 −100 μM) for 30 min in sulfuric acid condition (0.06 M) at room temperature, then fluorescence spectra were measured. As presented in Fig. 3 A, with the concentration of NO2 − increasing, the fluorescence intensity at 474 nm quenched gradually, but that of at 608 nm remained constant. Meanwhile, apparently distinguishable color variation from blue to red was also exhibited (Fig. 3B). The proposed probe in this study was a porous crystalline material, the diazotization reaction (between NO2 − and the amino group) occurred on the surface of the probe when the NO2 − in low concentrations. While with the NO2 − concentration increasing, the response center transferred to the cavity. Therefore, there were two fitted curves in Fig. 3 C, which was similar to other previously reported literature [34,35]. Fluorescence intensity ratio of F474/F608 showed good linear relationships against NO2 − concentration in the range of 0.5 to 10.0 μM (R2 = 0.9998) and 10.0 to 15.0 μM (R2 = 0.9973), respectively. The regression equations were fitted as Y1 = 3.5648 – 0.1951 C and Y2 = 2.8182 – 0.1128 C (Fig. 3 C), where Y is the ratio value of F474/F608, and C is the concentration of NO2 − (μM). Based on limit of detection (LOD) = 3σ/K (σ is the standard deviation of blank, K is the slope of the calibration plots), the proposed sensing platform provided an ultralow LOD of 0.3 μΜ. The World Health Organization (WHO) stipulates the maximum allowance of NO2 − in drinking water is 65.0 μM [36], so the probe is sensitive enough for NO2 − detection. Moreover, the color variations ignited by NO2 - were proved by the Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 3D). With the increase of NO2 − concentration, continuous color shift from blue to red was observed in the CIE chromaticity diagram, which provided the sensing basic for visual determination of NO2 − with a portable device.

To demonstrate the advantages of this ratiometric probe, MOF-NH2 with single emission at 474 nm was used to determine NO2 − as a comparison (Fig. S8). Although NO2 − led to decline of fluorescent intensity (Fig. S8A) and a linear relationship was achieved in the range of 0.5 to 10.0 μM (Fig. S8B), hardly discriminative blue color change from bright to dark was noticed (Fig. S8C), which was further confirmed by the coordinate points in CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. S8D). By contrast, the Ru@MOF-NH2 exhibited absolute advantages for visual determination of NO2 −.

Ratiometric fluorescent determination of NO2 − provided accurate results but required instrument of fluorophotometer, which limited its application in on-site determination. To address this problem, quantitative visual detection of NO2
− was carried out using smartphone camera and the installed color recognition App (Swatches). The R/B values, which were read out by identifying image color using Swatches App, increased with the increase of NO2 − concentration ( Fig. 4 A). Satisfactory linear correlation of R/B value versus NO2 − concentration was obtained within the range of 2 − 40 μM (R/B = 0.0491 C + 0.1359, R2 = 0.9868) with a LOD of 0.6 μΜ.

To evaluate the implementation results and the dependability, the semiquantitative determination with naked eye was completed by individual volunteers. As shown in Fig. 4B (top panel), the referenced color charts were first provided in accordance with the fluorescence pictures in Fig. 3 A. Next, random signal readout assays with naked eye were performed by 10 individual volunteers. Four samples with different contents of NO2 − (0, 6, 20, and 40 μΜ) were analyzed using Ru@MOF-NH2, then the naked eye readouts were conducted by the 10 individual volunteer users through comparing the solution color and the referenced color charts. The results reading out by all users are listed in Fig. 4B (bottom panel). The accuracy for analyzing some samples with specific NO2 − concentrations was determined to be 100%, suggesting the potential for detecting NO2 − contamination by the naked eye and quantitative visual sensing by portable device.

The sensing performance of Ru@MOF-NH2 for visual and quantitative determination of NO2
− was compared with other published methods (Fig. 4C and Table S1) [8–10,12,13,27,28,37–46]. The results showed that the visualization LOD and quantification LOD of the Ru@MOF-NH2 (red star) was much lower than or matched with that of other visual methods (blue circles) and instrumental methods (cyan, orange and green dots) with high practicability and reliability. Since the naked eye exhibits greater sensitivity to the color hue change than to the brightness change of a single color. Though lower or matched LODs can be achieved using some laboratory-instrument based methods, such as UV–vis (cyan dots), fluorescence (orange dots) and electrochemistry (green dots), only one-color change was produced in terms of visualization. In comparison, our method showed rich color change from blue to red with NO2 − increasing, which exhibited much superiority in terms of convenient manner of quantitative visualization.

The specificity of the proposed sensing platform was evaluated by analyzing samples containing other interference ions and species, including Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, Ni2+, glucose, ascorbic acid (AA) and some potential amino acids. Fig. 5A, B and Fig. S9A, S9B show the fluorescent ratio of F474/F608 and the R/B values toward NO2 − (100 μM) and other interferences (1.0 mM), respectively. From which we can see that only NO2 − caused dramatical changes on ratiometric signal and R/B values, whereas all other interference ions and substances led no perceivable change even with 10-fold NO2 −. Fig. 5C and Fig. S9C show the colorimetric performances of the sensing system towards NO2 − and interferences under 365 nm UV lamp. Except NO2 − led to obviously color change from blue to red, interferences caused no color change even with ten-fold of NO2 −. All these results promised the high selectivity of the proposed sensing platform to NO2 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the developed method was evaluated by analyzing lake water and ham sausage. Recovery tests were performed to evaluate the accuracy and repeatability of the method. Spiking samples with different amount of NO2 − were performed sample preparation and analysis procedures. The analysis results are presented in Table 1. The spiked recoveries were obtained based on the formula of (measured value – endogenous value)/added value × 100%. Both ratiometric fluorescent and visual strategies exhibited good recoveries and satisfactory coefficients of variation (CVs) for all the samples. In detail, the recoveries of ratiometric fluorescence detection model were in the range of 98–110%, and that of the quantitative visual detection model were in the range of 105–117%. The CVs were less than 2.3% and 4.3% for ratiometric fluorescence detection and quantitative visual detection, respectively. All these results clearly suggested that the proposed sensing platform based on the probe of Ru@MOF-NH2 has high practicability for NO2 − detection in complex matrix samples.

In general, a novel probe has been successfully developed by combing the MOF of MOF-NH2 with [Ru(bpy)3]2+ via one-pot approach for quantitative monitoring NO2 − contamination via ratiometric fluorescent signal or color hue recognition. The special diazo-reaction between the amino group of MOF-NH2 and NO2 − quenches the blue fluorescence of MOF-NH2 whereas the red fluorescence of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ remains constant the target-induced overlap of the blue fluorescence from MOF-NH2 reporter with the red of from [Ru(bpy)3]2+ reference leads to an obviously distinguishable color change from blue to red. Both signal outputs of ratiometric fluorescence and R/B values show high sensitivity and selectivity with as low as LODs of 0.3 μΜ and 0.6 μΜ. Moreover, the semiquantitative visual readout can fast distinguish the sample whether contaminate or not. Overall, this functionalized MOF sensor presents advantages of rapid and quantitative analysis NO2 − with both ratiometric fluorescence and color recognition in complex biological samples, demonstrating the potential for fast and accurately detecting small molecules by combining active-groups-functionalized-MOFs with [Ru(bpy)3]2+ through technologies of POCT.

Nitrite (NO2 −) is widely present in human environment, which acts as a primary indicator of water quality and food safety. However, excessive NO2 − in water and food is extremely harmful to public health, associating with the development of methemoglobinemia, accidental intoxications and even cancers. Therefore, we design a promising ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of “hazardous material” NO2 − by embedding [Ru(bpy)3] 2 + into an amino functionalized MOF (MOF-NH2) through a facile one-pot synthesis approach. Using a smartphone-based analyzer, ultrasensitive quantitative visual analysis of NO2 − is achieved by reading R/B values.

Lian Xia: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rong-Mei Kong: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiaoling Sun: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yifan Gao: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Fengli Qu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Maosheng Liang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by grants awarded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174086, 22074080), Outstanding Taishan Scholar of Shandong Province (tsqn202211131), Natural Science Foundation Projects of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB056, ZR2020KB020), Outstanding Youth Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017JL010) and Changjiang Scholar Program of the Ministry of Education of China (Q2019258), Open Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (2021014).

ce:section.ce:para: All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.134021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
